complete sentences. Articles are often left out, especially in the names of Titles, labels, headings, notices and slogans usually consist of short phrases, not 4 titles,    notices,     etc chance there - and it's a goal!

And the score still Spurs 1, Arsenal 3 ... that's Pearce ... Pearce kind of grammar. Auxiliaries and other less important verbs are often left out.

Commentaries on fast-moving events like football matches also have their own 3 commentaries Cheers, Ally new date for next week.

Tell Dan can't manage meeting, papers attached, will fix Hi Joe, Subject      meeting Cc joe@gcom.org.netTo grammar •  292  headlines TROOPS     FOR   GLASGOW?

(= Are soldiers going to be sent to Glasgow?)              -> For is also used to refer to future movements or plans.

PM  TO  VISIT   AUSTRALIA       HOSPITALS     TO  TAKE  FEWER   PATIENTS f  Headlines often use infinitives to refer to the future.

So it is not always easy to e  Many headline words are used as both nouns and verbs, and nouns are often BRITAIN   GETTING   WARMER,    SAY   SCIENTISTS     TRADE   FIGURES    IMPROVING The  present progressive is used to talk about changes. Be is usually dropped.

BLIND  GIRL  CLIMBS   EVEREST (= . . . has climbed . . .) STUDENTS     FIGHT  FOR   COURSE    CHANGES (=  . . . are fighting . . .) perfect forms. The  simple present is used for both present and past events.

d  In headlines, simple tenses are often used instead of progressive or SCHOOLBOY      WALKS   IN SPACE SHAKESPEARE     PLAY  IMMORAL    SAYS   HEADMASTER c   Headlines often leave out articles and the verb be.

CUT            PAY    FACTORY           FURNITURE.

It sometimes helps to read FURNITURE     FACTORY    PAY  CUT   PROTEST the string modify those that follow.

b  Headlines often contain strings of three, four or more nouns; nouns earlier in EXETER    MAN'S  DOUBLE   MARRIAGE    BID MORE   WAGE   CUTS    LUXURY      HOTEL   DEATH phrases with no verb.

a  Headlines are not always complete sentences. Many headlines consist of noun 2 grammar grammar, and words are often used in unusual ways.

very different from ordinary English. In this style there are some special rules of One reason for this is that headlines are often written in a special style, which  is TREASURE).

English news headlines can be very difficult to understand.

Headlines are the short titles above news reports (e.g.

1  special     language 292 headlines Written Texts   Section 26 grammar •  292  headlines BA MAKES   RECORD    LOSS BA                   British Airways SMALL   SCHOOLS     FACE  AXE COUNTRY     BUS   SERVICES    AXED axe                 abolish, close down; abolition, closure MP  TO  APPEAR  ON  DRUGS    CHARGES appear           appear in court accused  of a  crime WOMAN    ALLEGES   UNFAIR   TREATMENT allege             make an accusation FLOOD   ALERT   ON  EAST   COAST alert               alarm, warning UNIONS   AID HOSPITAL    STRIKERS MORE   AID FOR   POOR   COUNTRIES aid                   military or financial help; to help FOOD   CRISIS:  GOVERNMENT     TO  ACT act                   take action; do something The  following is a list of common headline vocabulary.
